title: Introduction to Ansible
learningObjectives: Understand what Ansible is and its agentless architecture
Identify control node vs managed nodes
Install ansible-core 2.19 on a Linux control node
Understand the Data Tagging feature new in 2.19

theory.content:
What is Ansible?

Ansible is an open-source IT automation platform developed by Red Hat. Unlike other configuration management tools, Ansible is agentless — it communicates with managed nodes over SSH (Linux) or WinRM (Windows) without installing any software on them.

Architecture

Ansible uses a control node (where Ansible is installed) and managed nodes (target machines). The control node pushes configurations via SSH. All automation logic lives in YAML files called playbooks.

Key Components

  • Inventory: A list of managed hosts (INI or YAML format)
  • Modules: Units of work (e.g., ansible.builtin.copy, ansible.builtin.ping)
  • Tasks: Single calls to a module
  • Playbook: An ordered list of plays containing tasks
  • Role: A reusable, structured set of tasks, variables, and files

Installation (ansible-core 2.19)

# On RHEL/CentOS 9+
sudo dnf install ansible-core

# On Ubuntu/Debian
sudo apt-add-repository ppa:ansible/ansible
sudo apt install ansible-core

# Via pip (recommended for version pinning)
pip install ansible-core==2.19

# Or install the community ansible package (bundles ansible-core 2.19)
pip install ansible==11.*

Verify: ansible --version should report ansible [core 2.19.x]

What is New in ansible-core 2.19?

  • Data Tagging (major architectural feature): A fundamental overhaul of how Ansible tracks data provenance. Every variable and return value now carries metadata tags (origin, trust level, deprecation status) throughout execution. This enables: (1) marking variables as deprecated so accessing them emits an actionable warning with the suggested replacement; (2) trust-based data handling — values from untrusted sources can be isolated; (3) improved debuggability by tracing exactly where a value came from. This is the largest internal change to Ansible's data model in several major releases.
  • --flush-cache option: Added to ansible, ansible-console, and ansible-pull for clearing the fact cache on demand.
  • Windows Server 2025 support: ansible-core 2.19 officially supports WinRM and PSRP connections to Windows Server 2025 targets.
  • Traceback control: Use ANSIBLE_DISPLAY_TRACEBACK=all to see full tracebacks for any error, warning, or deprecation — useful for debugging custom modules.
  • Undefined vars in vars_files now warn: Previously silently ignored; 2.19 emits an explicit warning.

First Ad-Hoc Command

ansible localhost -m ansible.builtin.ping -c local

This pings localhost using the built-in ping module with a local connection (no SSH needed).
